skill.name: onboard
skill.description: Design or improve onboarding flows, empty states, guided tours, and first-time user experiences for digital products.
skill.summary: Create onboarding experiences that help users understand value quickly and complete their first successful action.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, browser storage, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found the command, crypto, and reconnaissance hits are markdown or natural-language false positives. LocalStorage appears only as benign onboarding-state guidance, so the skill is safe to publish with a minor privacy caution.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported backtick execution locations are markdown code fences or inline UI examples, not executable Ruby or shell commands. No evidence found of command execution instructions or user-controlled shell input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are visible as markdown delimiters or inline keyboard and help text. They do not invoke a shell, interpreter, or external process.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Guidance Stores Only Onboarding State
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for sensitive data exposure. The LocalStorage examples store completion and tooltip-seen flags only. This is not credential storage, but implementers should avoid storing personal data or secrets in browser storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use fixed onboarding keys with boolean-like values. There is no evidence of tokens, credentials, profile data, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Natural-Language False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography detector appears to match text such as design-related wording, headings, and UX guidance. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA-1, encryption code, hashing code, or cryptographic configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is a prose design guide and contains no cryptographic API usage. The reported lines are headings or UX instructions.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Validation Guidance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported line asks designers to validate whether a user completed an onboarding task correctly. No evidence found of host enumeration, system probing, or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is a product onboarding checklist item. It is unrelated to system reconnaissance or local machine inspection.

content.value_statement: New users often miss product value when setup, empty states, and tours are unclear. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design focused onboarding that reaches value quickly.
actual_capabilities: Assess onboarding goals, user experience level, and the target aha moment.
Design welcome screens, setup flows, first-success moments, and skip paths.
Improve empty states with value framing, examples, templates, and clear calls to action.
Plan contextual tooltips, guided tours, feature announcements, and progressive onboarding.
Recommend implementation patterns for tooltips, tours, modals, progress tracking, and analytics.
Define quality checks such as completion rate, comprehension, skip rate, and time to value.
limitations: Requires product context, target users, and the desired first success moment.
Does not provide a full analytics implementation or production instrumentation code.
Does not replace usability testing with real users.
Assumes another design-context skill may be available when following its preparation guidance.

use_cases.title: Launch a New Product Flow
use_cases.description: Create a short first-run experience that explains value, gathers only required setup data, and guides users to first success.

use_cases.title: Improve Empty States
use_cases.description: Turn blank or confusing pages into useful starting points with examples, templates, and clear next actions.

use_cases.title: Introduce Complex Features
use_cases.description: Plan contextual tooltips or guided tours that teach advanced workflows without blocking experienced users.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Onboarding Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing first-run experience.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the onboarding flow for [product or feature]. The target users are [user type]. Identify what should change to reach the aha moment faster.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Empty State
prompt_templates.scenario: Design a useful empty state for a product area.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an empty state for [screen or feature]. Include what will appear here, why it matters, and the best first action.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Guided Tour
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a short tour for a complex or newly changed interface.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a guided tour for [workflow]. Keep it short, optional, and focused on actions users can take immediately.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Adoption Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for staged onboarding and measurement.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an onboarding strategy for [product area]. Include progressive education, skip paths, completion tracking, and quality metrics.

output_examples.input: Improve onboarding for a project management app with no projects yet.
output_examples.output: A revised empty state that explains project value, offers a template, provides one primary create action, and includes optional help.

output_examples.input: Design onboarding for a complex reporting dashboard.
output_examples.output: A short welcome step, sample data, a guided first report task, contextual tips, and metrics for completion and time to value.

output_examples.input: Reduce repeated tooltips for returning users.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to track dismissed tips, respect skip choices, and show help again only from a user-controlled help menu.

best_practices: Start with the user goal, the aha moment, and the minimum learning needed for first success.
Make onboarding optional where possible and provide clear skip or replay controls.
Teach features in context with real actions, examples, and progressive disclosure.
anti_patterns: Do not force long onboarding before users can access the product.
Do not explain every feature upfront when users only need the next useful action.
Do not repeat dismissed tours or tooltips after users have chosen to skip them.

faq.question: What kind of onboarding can this skill design?
faq.answer: It can help design welcome flows, setup steps, empty states, tooltips, guided tours, and first-success experiences.

faq.question: Does this skill write production code?
faq.answer: It mainly provides product and UX guidance. It may suggest implementation patterns, but it is not a complete coding framework.

faq.question: What context should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the feature, target users, their experience level, the desired aha moment, and any current drop-off points.

faq.question: Can experienced users skip onboarding?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance strongly favors optional onboarding, clear skip controls, and replayable help.

faq.question: How does it handle empty states?
faq.answer: It frames what will appear, why it matters, how to start, and what examples or templates can reduce friction.

faq.question: How should teams measure onboarding quality?
faq.answer: Use completion rate, comprehension, skip rate, desired next action, and time to value.
